```json
{
  "module": "infrastructure",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 62,
    "domain_depth": 55,
    "data_richness": 50,
    "interaction_quality": 58,
    "ai_integration": 45,
    "mobile_first": 48,
    "performance": 65,
    "typescript_quality": 68,
    "error_handling": 70,
    "realtime": 62,
    "accessibility": 40,
    "security": 60,
    "differentiation": 52,
    "enterprise_readiness": 58
  },
  "overall_score": 57,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C1-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "accessibility",
      "title": "App shell missing skip-to-content target and ARIA landmarks",
      "file": "src/App.tsx",
      "description": "SkipToContent component is imported and rendered but the main content area lacks an id='main-content' target and role='main' landmark. Screen reader users cannot skip navigation. The sidebar also lacks role='navigation' and aria-label.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes in the main layout JSX where Sidebar and page content are rendered: BEFORE: The main content wrapper div has no id or role attributes, and the Sidebar wrapper has no navigation landmark. AFTER: 1. Add id='main-content' and role='main' and aria-label='Page content' to the div that wraps the <Routes> / page content area. 2. Wrap the <Sidebar> component in a <nav aria-label='Main navigation'>. 3. Ensure the <SkipToContent> component targets href='#main-content'. These are purely attribute additions to existing JSX elements, no structural changes. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C1-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "mobile_first",
      "title": "No responsive breakpoint handling for main layout grid",
      "file": "src/App.tsx",
      "description": "The main layout uses a fixed sidebar width (240px) with no CSS adaptation for mobile viewports. On screens under 768px, the sidebar overlaps content or content is pushed off screen. MobileLayout component exists but the conditional rendering based on useMediaQuery may not cover the main authenticated layout shell properly.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/App.tsx, in the authenticated layout section where Sidebar and main content are rendered side by side: BEFORE: The layout container uses a fixed inline style like display:'flex' with the sidebar always visible at 240px width regardless of viewport. AFTER: 1. Use the existing useMediaQuery hook to detect isMobile (max-width: 768px). 2. When isMobile is true, hide the desktop Sidebar and render the MobileLayout component with bottom navigation instead. 3. When isMobile is false, render the current desktop layout with Sidebar + content. 4. On the main content div, set style to marginLeft: isMobile ? 0 : (sidebarCollapsed ? 64 : 240), transition: 'margin-left 150ms ease-out'. This is a conditional rendering change using existing components, not new components. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C1-003",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "error_handling",
      "title": "Lazy route loading has no user-visible fallback on chunk failure",
      "file": "src/App.tsx",
      "description": "lazyWithRetry silently retries 3 times then throws. The Suspense fallback is a basic Skeleton but chunk load failures (network issues, deployments) show a blank ErrorBoundary with no actionable recovery. Users see a white screen with no way to retry or navigate.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/App.tsx, in the ErrorBoundary component that wraps the Routes: BEFORE: ErrorBoundary catches chunk load errors but renders a generic fallback or potentially nothing useful. The user has no way to retry loading the failed chunk. AFTER: 1. In the ErrorBoundary fallback render, check if the error message includes 'Loading chunk' or 'dynamically imported module' or 'Failed to fetch'. 2. If it is a chunk error, render a centered card with: an icon (RefreshCw from lucide-react), heading 'Page failed to load', body text 'A new version may be available. Click below to reload.', and a button labeled 'Reload Page' that calls window.location.reload(). 3. Style using existing theme tokens (colors.surface, colors.text, spacing.lg, borderRadius.lg). 4. Add role='alert' to the error container. This modifies only the ErrorBoundary fallback rendering logic. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C1-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "performance",
      "title": "All overlay panels loaded eagerly via Suspense without condition",
      "file": "src/App.tsx",
      "description": "AIContextPanel, FloatingAIButton, CopilotPanel, NotificationCenter, ShortcutOverlay, and ExportCenter are lazy-loaded but always rendered inside Suspense boundaries regardless of whether they are open. This triggers chunk downloads on first app load even when panels are closed, adding unnecessary network requests and parse time.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/App.tsx, in the section where overlay panels (CopilotPanel, NotificationCenter, ShortcutOverlay, ExportCenter, AIContextPanel) are rendered: BEFORE: All lazy overlay components are rendered inside Suspense boundaries unconditionally on every page load, causing their chunks to download immediately. AFTER: 1. For each overlay panel, wrap the Suspense+component in a conditional: only render when the panel's open state is true. Use the existing store hooks: useCopilotStore().isOpen for CopilotPanel, useUiStore() for notification/shortcut/export panel states. 2. Pattern: {copilotOpen && <Suspense fallback={null}><CopilotPanel /></Suspense>}. 3. For FloatingAIButton, keep it always rendered since it is a small trigger button. 4. This ensures chunks are only downloaded when the user actually opens a panel. No new imports needed, just conditional rendering around existing JSX. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C1-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "security",
      "title": "API router pathMatches function vulnerable to path traversal edge cases",
      "file": "src/api/v1/router.ts",
      "description": "The pathMatches function splits on '/' and compares segment count, but does not normalize paths. Trailing slashes, double slashes, or encoded segments could bypass route matching, causing resolveRequiredScope to return undefined (no route found) instead of the correct scope, potentially allowing unauthenticated access if the caller treats undefined as 'no auth needed'.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/v1/router.ts, in the pathMatches function: BEFORE: The function splits pattern and actual paths on '/' and compares directly without normalization. Paths like '/api/v1/webhooks/' (trailing slash) or '/api/v1//webhooks' (double slash) would fail to match the registered pattern '/api/v1/webhooks'. AFTER: 1. At the start of pathMatches, normalize both pattern and actual by: removing trailing slashes, collapsing consecutive slashes, and decoding URI components. Add: const normalize = (p: string) => decodeURIComponent(p).replace(/\\/+/g, '/').replace(/\\/$/, '') || '/'; then use const patternParts = normalize(pattern).split('/') and const actualParts = normalize(actual).split('/'). 2. Also in resolveRequiredScope, normalize the incoming path before the find() call using the same normalize function. Extract normalize as a named function above pathMatches. 3. This is a pure logic change in two functions in one file. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's routing and app shell are mature with proper ARIA landmarks, responsive layouts, and graceful error recovery. SiteSync's infrastructure layer has solid patterns (scoped client, error hierarchy, query key factory, realtime invalidation) that are architecturally stronger than Procore's legacy approach, but the app shell has accessibility and mobile gaps that would fail enterprise procurement accessibility audits. The versioned API router pattern with scope resolution is a differentiator that Procore lacks in their client SDK.",
  "summary": "The Infrastructure & Routing module has strong architectural foundations: typed error hierarchy, scoped Supabase client with automatic project isolation, centralized query key factory, realtime invalidation mapping, and a versioned API router with scope resolution. These patterns are well-designed for enterprise scale. However, the app shell has five impactful gaps: missing ARIA landmarks and skip-to-content targets (failing WCAG compliance), no responsive layout adaptation for mobile viewports (sidebar overlaps content), chunk load failures showing blank screens with no recovery action, all overlay panel chunks downloading eagerly on every page load (unnecessary ~200KB+ of JS), and a path normalization vulnerability in the API router that could bypass scope checks. All five issues are surgical fixes to existing code with no new features required."
}
```
